value: Hypertension is a chronic medical condition characterised by persistently elevated blood pressure in the arteries. Most cases are classified as primary (or essential) hypertension, which has no identifiable cause and tends to develop over many years, while a smaller proportion are due to an underlying condition (secondary hypertension).

Hypertension is extremely common, affecting over a quarter of adults globally, and is a leading risk factor for premature death. It is well-documented in primary care, hospital, and insurance claims data, making it readily identifiable in most real-world data sources.
55 to 65 (The incidence of hypertension increases steadily with age, with most new diagnoses occurring in middle-aged and older adults.)
45% to 55% (At younger ages, hypertension is more common in men, but after menopause, the incidence in women increases and may exceed that of men in older age groups.)

85% to 95% (While hypertension itself is not directly fatal, it is a major risk factor for life-threatening events such as heart attacks and strokes, leading to a modest reduction in long-term survival compared to the general population.)

Expect to see overlap with type 2 diabetes (20-40%), hospitalised inpatients (30-60%), users of warfarin (5-15%), users of acetaminophen (70-90%), users of morphine (10-25%), and, among males, measurement of prostate specific antigen level (40-70%).

The diagnosis of hypertension often occurs concurrently with or precedes the diagnosis of type 2 diabetes, and typically precedes hospitalisation or the initiation of warfarin or morphine by several years. Acetaminophen use is sporadic and can occur at any time, while prostate specific antigen screening often begins around the same age that hypertension is diagnosed.

type 2 diabetes mellitus, dyslipidaemia, obesity, chronic kidney disease, ischaemic heart disease, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, stroke, peripheral arterial disease, and gout are the most common comorbidities. (These conditions often share common underlying risk factors with hypertension, such as metabolic syndrome, or are direct consequences of long-term damage caused by elevated blood pressure.)

headache, dizziness, visual disturbances, chest pain, dyspnoea, palpitations, epistaxis, haematuria, tinnitus, and fatigue are the most common signs and symptoms. (Hypertension is frequently asymptomatic and discovered during routine screening, but these symptoms may arise with very high blood pressure levels or as a result of end-organ damage.)

ramipril, amlodipine, indapamide, losartan, bisoprolol, doxazosin, atorvastatin, aspirin, metformin, and furosemide are the most common medications. (This list includes first and second-line antihypertensive drugs from various classes, as well as medications commonly prescribed for concurrent conditions like dyslipidaemia and type 2 diabetes.)

Type 2 diabetes is a chronic metabolic disorder characterised by hyperglycaemia resulting from the body's ineffective use of insulin (insulin resistance) and a relative deficiency in insulin production.
Type 2 diabetes is a very common condition globally, and we would expect to see a high number of cases in primary care, hospital, and insurance claims data.
45 to 65 (The condition typically develops in middle-aged and older adults, although it is increasingly being diagnosed in younger individuals.)
50% to 55% (There is a slight male predominance in the prevalence of type 2 diabetes.)
85% to 95% (Mortality is higher than in the general population, primarily due to an increased risk of cardiovascular disease.)

Expect to see a high overlap with hypertension (40-70%) and user_of_acetaminophen (30-60%); a moderate overlap with hospitalised_inpatient (10-30%) and measurement_of_prostate_specific_antigen_level (10-20%); and a lower overlap with user_of_warfarin (5-15%) and user_of_morphine (5-15%).

For people also in the other cohorts, the type_2_diabetes diagnosis will typically precede entry into the hospitalised_inpatient, user_of_warfarin, and user_of_morphine cohorts by several years; diagnosis of hypertension often occurs around the same time as or just before the diabetes diagnosis; timing relative to user_of_acetaminophen is variable; and diabetes diagnosis will typically precede the first measurement_of_prostate_specific_antigen_level.

the most common comorbidities are hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, obesity, chronic kidney disease, ischaemic heart disease, heart failure, peripheral vascular disease, atrial fibrillation, diabetic retinopathy, and diabetic neuropathy. (These conditions often share common risk factors with type 2 diabetes, such as obesity, or are long-term complications of the disease.)

the most common signs and symptoms are polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, blurred vision, polyphagia, numbness or tingling in the hands or feet, slow-healing sores, frequent infections, unexplained weight loss, and acanthosis nigricans. (These features are a direct result of high blood glucose levels and the body's inability to use glucose for energy effectively.)

the most common medications are metformin, gliclazide, sitagliptin, dapagliflozin, ramipril, atorvastatin, insulin, aspirin, bisoprolol, and liraglutide. (These drugs are used to manage blood glucose, blood pressure, and cholesterol to reduce the risk of long-term complications.)

A hospitalised inpatient is an individual who has been formally admitted to a hospital for medical care, diagnosis, or treatment that requires at least one overnight stay. The reasons for admission are highly diverse, spanning the full spectrum of acute illnesses, exacerbations of chronic conditions, scheduled surgical procedures, and diagnostic investigations.

Hospital admission is a very common event, particularly among the elderly and those with chronic diseases. Cases are readily and comprehensively identified in hospital administrative data, electronic health records, and insurance claims data, but may be less visible in primary care data without integrated systems.

65 to 75 (The age distribution of hospitalised inpatients is typically bimodal, with peaks in neonates and older adults, but the median age is high due to the large volume of admissions in the elderly.)
48% to 52% (The proportion of males is often close to balanced, though it can vary depending on the specific hospital setting and the types of admissions most frequently seen.)
60% to 80% (Survival following hospitalisation is highly variable and is primarily dependent on the patient's age, the reason for admission, and the burden of underlying comorbidities.)

Expect high overlap with users of acetaminophen (60-80%) and people with hypertension (40-60%) or type 2 diabetes (20-40%), and moderate overlap with users of morphine (15-30%), users of warfarin (5-15%), and those with a measurement of prostate specific antigen level (5-15%).

Diagnoses of chronic conditions like hypertension and type 2 diabetes will typically predate the hospitalisation by many years, as will the associated long-term medication use such as warfarin and screening tests like prostate specific antigen measurement. In contrast, use of medications for acute symptom control, such as acetaminophen or morphine, is very often initiated at the time of or during the hospital admission.

hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, ischaemic heart disease, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, asthma, dementia, and cancer (These common chronic conditions increase the risk of acute illness requiring hospitalisation and often complicate the management of the admitting condition.)

dyspnoea, chest pain, abdominal pain, fever, altered mental state, syncope, weakness, nausea, vomiting, and falls (These are common, often non-specific, presenting features of a wide range of acute medical and surgical conditions that are severe enough to warrant hospital admission.)

paracetamol, atorvastatin, ramipril, aspirin, bisoprolol, furosemide, omeprazole, metformin, salbutamol, and apixaban (This list includes medications for managing common chronic comorbidities as well as drugs frequently used in hospital to manage acute symptoms like pain and fever.)

Warfarin is an oral anticoagulant medication used to prevent the formation of blood clots. It is most commonly prescribed for individuals with atrial fibrillation, venous thromboembolism (including deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism), or those with mechanical heart valves.
Warfarin is a frequently prescribed medication, and users can be readily identified in both primary care electronic health records and insurance claims data through prescription records.
65 to 75 (The most common indications for warfarin, such as atrial fibrillation and venous thromboembolism, increase in incidence with age.)
50% to 60% (The primary indications for warfarin therapy, particularly atrial fibrillation, are slightly more common in men.)
45% to 65% (Mortality is driven by the underlying cardiovascular conditions for which warfarin is prescribed and the advanced age of many patients, rather than the medication itself.)

Overlap with hypertension will be very high (70-90%), high with hospitalised_inpatient (40-60%), and moderate with type_2_diabetes (20-40%), user_of_acetaminophen (30-50%), measurement_of_prostate_specific_antigen_level (20-40% of males), and user_of_morphine (10-25%).

Hypertension and type_2_diabetes will typically be diagnosed years before warfarin initiation. Hospitalisation often occurs concurrently with or immediately precedes the start of warfarin for an acute event, while other cohort entries have a less distinct temporal relationship.

hypertension, atrial fibrillation, ischaemic heart disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, heart failure, chronic kidney disease, stroke, dyslipidaemia, peripheral vascular disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (These conditions are common as they are risk factors for, or consequences of, the thromboembolic diseases for which warfarin is indicated.)

bruising, bleeding, dyspnoea, palpitations, chest pain, oedema, fatigue, haematuria, epistaxis, syncope (These are typically related to the underlying cardiovascular condition requiring anticoagulation or are a direct side effect of warfarin's anticoagulant properties.)

ramipril, bisoprolol, atorvastatin, furosemide, amlodipine, aspirin, lansoprazole, metformin, digoxin, spironolactone (These medications are commonly used to manage the underlying cardiovascular diseases and comorbidities frequently seen in patients taking warfarin.)
Acetaminophen is a widely used medication with analgesic and antipyretic properties. It is used to treat mild to moderate pain and to reduce fever from a wide variety of causes.

Use of acetaminophen is extremely common across the global population, both as an over-the-counter and prescribed medication, and will be readily identifiable in primary care, hospital, and insurance claims data.

40 to 60 (While acetaminophen is used by people of all ages, from infants to the elderly, the median age in a general user cohort often reflects the age of individuals with chronic pain conditions like osteoarthritis.)
40% to 55% (Use is common in both sexes, although a slight female predominance may be observed due to a higher prevalence of some pain conditions such as migraine and fibromyalgia in women.)

90% to 98% (Survival is generally very high and similar to the general population, as acetaminophen is typically used for non-life-threatening conditions, though it may be lower in those with severe underlying disease.)

Expect to see overlap with hypertension (20-40%), type 2 diabetes (10-25%), hospitalised inpatient (15-30%), user of warfarin (5-15%), user of morphine (80-95%), and measurement of prostate specific antigen level (10-25% of the total cohort).

For chronic conditions like hypertension and type 2 diabetes, their diagnosis often precedes or is concurrent with regular acetaminophen use by months to years. For hospitalised inpatients, acetaminophen use typically begins at the start of the admission. Acetaminophen use often precedes the initiation of morphine by weeks to years but can be started concurrently for acute severe pain. The timing relative to warfarin use or prostate specific antigen testing is variable and depends on the specific clinical context.

osteoarthritis, hypertension, headache, type 2 diabetes mellitus, back pain, upper respiratory tract infection, chronic pain syndrome, influenza, fibromyalgia, musculoskeletal pain (These conditions are either direct reasons for needing pain relief or are common chronic diseases prevalent in the adult population who are frequent users of analgesics.)

pain, fever, headache, myalgia, arthralgia, backache, sore throat, pyrexia, toothache, malaise (These represent the primary indications for which acetaminophen is used, reflecting its function as a first-line analgesic and antipyretic medication.)

ibuprofen, codeine, amlodipine, lisinopril, metformin, atorvastatin, omeprazole, amoxicillin, aspirin, naproxen (This list includes other commonly used analgesics, often co-prescribed or available over-the-counter, and medications used to treat frequent underlying comorbidities.)

Morphine is a potent opioid analgesic used to manage severe acute or chronic pain that is not adequately controlled by other analgesics. Its use is common following major surgery, for severe trauma, and in the management of pain associated with advanced cancer.

Users of morphine are commonly seen in healthcare data, particularly from hospital settings (for acute pain) and specialist palliative care or pain clinics (for chronic pain). Cases are readily identifiable in both electronic healthcare records and claims data where prescriptions are recorded.

55 to 75 (The age of individuals using morphine is highly variable, but its use is most concentrated in older adults due to the higher prevalence of cancer, major surgeries, and severe chronic degenerative conditions in this population.)
45% to 55% (The proportion of males and females is expected to be roughly equal, as the underlying conditions requiring potent analgesia do not have a strong overall sex predilection.)

30% to 60% (Survival is extremely dependent on the reason for prescription, ranging from very high for post-operative use to very low for use in end-of-life care, which significantly lowers the overall cohort survival rate.)

Expect to see very high overlap with hospitalised_inpatient (50-80%) and user_of_acetaminophen (60-90%), high overlap with hypertension (30-50%) and type 2_diabetes (20-40%), moderate overlap with measurement_of_prostate_specific_antigen_level (15-30% of males), and lower overlap with user_of_warfarin (5-15%).

For people in the user_of_morphine cohort, entry into the hypertension, type 2_diabetes, user_of_warfarin, and measurement_of_prostate_specific_antigen_level cohorts would typically precede morphine use by several years. Entry into the hospitalised_inpatient cohort is often concurrent or immediately precedes morphine initiation (within days), while use of acetaminophen may precede or be concurrent.

The most common comorbidities are hypertension, cancer, osteoarthritis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, type 2 diabetes, depression, chronic kidney disease, anxiety, constipation, and substance use disorder. (These conditions are frequently seen as they are either the underlying cause of severe pain (e.g., cancer, osteoarthritis) or are common age-related comorbidities in the population typically requiring morphine.)

The most common signs and symptoms are pain, constipation, nausea, somnolence, vomiting, dizziness, pruritus, confusion, dry mouth, and respiratory depression. (These represent a combination of the primary indication for the drug (pain) and the well-established adverse effects of opioid analgesics on the central nervous and gastrointestinal systems.)

The most common medications are laxatives, antiemetics, paracetamol, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, gabapentinoids, benzodiazepines, antidepressants, proton pump inhibitors, anticoagulants, and diuretics. (This list includes medications to manage the side effects of morphine (laxatives, antiemetics), provide multi-modal analgesia (paracetamol), and treat common comorbidities found in this patient population.)

Measurement of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) level is a blood test primarily used for screening and monitoring prostate cancer. Elevated levels can indicate malignancy but are also commonly caused by benign conditions such as benign prostatic hyperplasia or prostatitis.
Prostate-specific antigen testing is a very common procedure, particularly in primary care for men over 50, and is therefore readily identifiable in most types of real-world healthcare data.
55 to 65 (PSA testing is most common in men over 50 due to the increased risk of prostate conditions, such as benign prostatic hyperplasia and prostate cancer, with age.)
100% to 100% (The prostate-specific antigen test is exclusively performed on males as the prostate gland is part of the male reproductive system.)
90% to 98% (As PSA testing is often performed for screening in asymptomatic men, the five-year survival of this cohort is high and reflects that of the general male population of a similar age.)

Expect to see a 30-50% overlap with hypertension, 10-25% with type 2 diabetes, 5-15% with hospitalised inpatient, 5-10% with user of warfarin, 40-60% with user of acetaminophen, and 1-5% with user of morphine.

For people also in the other cohorts, entry into the hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and user of warfarin cohorts would typically precede the PSA measurement by several years, whereas entry into the hospitalised inpatient and user of morphine cohorts would often occur weeks to years after an abnormal PSA result, with no typical timing relationship for the user of acetaminophen cohort.

the most common comorbidities are hypertension, benign prostatic hyperplasia, hyperlipidaemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus, ischaemic heart disease, and obesity. (The most common comorbidities are those prevalent in the older male population for whom PSA testing is most indicated.)

the most common signs and symptoms are nocturia, urinary frequency, weak urinary stream, urinary urgency, dysuria, and haematuria. (These symptoms are commonly associated with prostatic conditions like benign prostatic hyperplasia or prostate cancer, which are primary indications for PSA testing.)

the most common medications are alpha-adrenoceptor antagonists, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, statins, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, beta blocking agents, and metformin. (Common medications reflect the management of age-related comorbidities and specific urological conditions like benign prostatic hyperplasia.)
